did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a modified Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on GoogLeNet (Inception architecture) with an added deconvolution layer to perform binary classification of benign and malignant melanoma using dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a modified GoogLeNet CNN for melanoma diagnosis. It details the methodology (modifying GoogLeNet with a deconvolution layer), the dataset used (ISIC subset of 21,570 images), training parameters, and reports new empirical results (classification accuracy of 0.9309, time complexity reduction) compared against baseline models (AlexNet, ResNet-50). Although it is an extended version of a previous conference paper, it contains new experimental validation and results, fitting the definition of a research paper.

title: A modified version of GoogLeNet for melanoma diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes a modified GoogLeNet convolutional neural network with an added deconvolution layer to improve the differential diagnosis of benign and malignant melanoma. The model was trained and evaluated on a subset of 21,570 dermoscopy images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, comprising 19,373 benign and 2,197 malignant cases. The authors compared the performance of their proposed CNN against baseline architectures including AlexNet, original GoogLeNet, and ResNet-50 using eight metrics: classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, kappa, F-measure, polygon area metric, and time complexity. The proposed model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 0.9309 and reduced training time complexity from 283 minutes 50 seconds to 256 minutes 26 seconds compared to the original GoogLeNet. These results indicate that the modified architecture offers superior accuracy and efficiency for melanoma classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: 19,373 benign dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset used for binary classification.
2,197 malignant dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset used for binary classification.

The paper mentions classifying 'benign and malignant skin lesions' generally in the context of the ISIC dataset and future work, but the specific experiments and counts provided are strictly for benign and malignant melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopy images into 'benign' and 'malignant' classes using a modified GoogLeNet CNN. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) with a classification accuracy of 0.9309. The last softmax layer was modified to output 2 classes.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public archive of clinical and dermoscopic images of skin lesions from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration: Melanoma Project. The study used a subset of 21,570 images selected from the 23,906 images available in the full dataset.

use.training: Used for training the modified GoogLeNet CNN model (80% of the subset was used for training).
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the subset was used for validation).
use.other: Used for performance comparison against baseline models (AlexNet, original GoogLeNet, ResNet-50) and previous study results.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the overall performance of the proposed CNN against baseline models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet-50) by aggregating classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, kappa, and F-measure.
To provide a detailed visual comparison of CNN performances across multiple metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, kappa, F-measure, polygon area metric, and time complexity) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Subset of 21,570 images: 1.experiment: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant melanoma using modified GoogLeNet and baseline models
ISIC Dataset (Subset of 21,570 images: 1.notes: The proposed CNN achieved the best classification accuracy, sensitivity, kappa, F-measure, and J scores. ResNet-50 achieved the best PAM and AUC scores. The proposed CNN reduced time complexity compared to original GoogLeNet and was significantly faster than ResNet-50. All models reached these scores at 5 epochs with 8625 iterations.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; used as a baseline architecture for comparison with AlexNet and ResNet-50, implying potential transfer learning or standard initialization, but specific pretraining weights are not detailed.

architecture_details.modification: A 3x3 deconvolution (transposed convolution) layer was inserted after the last convolution layer of the GoogLeNet model to perform upsampling and preserve high-level information.
architecture_details.inception_modules: Contains inception modules with 1x1, 3x3, 5x5 convolution sublayers and 3x3 max pooling operations.
architecture_details.pooling: Max pooling between blocks; average pooling layer at the end of the network (standard GoogLeNet structure).
architecture_details.layers: Not explicitly counted, but described as a deeper and wider CNN compared to AlexNet.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (used MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox)
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce GT 750M GPU, 2.40 GHz 8 Core Intel 7 4700HQ CPU, 16GB RAM
training_details.software: MATLAB R2020b with Deep Learning Toolbox

classification_or_output_head.modifications: The last softmax layer outputs were modified to 2 classes (benign vs. malignant).

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224 x 224 pixels using bicubic interpolation to preserve details
Basic data augmentation applied to balance benign, malignant, and other classes
Reduction of image size to fit CNN input layers

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the whole ISIC dataset to balance benign, malignant, and other classes. Specific geometric or color transform parameters were not reported; the primary purpose was class balancing rather than accuracy improvement.
Images were resized to 224 x 224 pixels using bicubic interpolation to preserve details and fit the CNN input layers.

conclusions: The proposed modified GoogLeNet CNN, which includes a 3x3 deconvolution layer inserted after the last convolution layer, achieved the highest classification accuracy (0.9309) among the tested models (AlexNet, original GoogLeNet, ResNet-50) for binary classification of benign and malignant melanoma.
The proposed model demonstrated superior performance in sensitivity (0.9739), kappa (0.9620), and F-measure (0.9620) compared to the baseline architectures.

The modified architecture reduced the training time complexity to 256 minutes 26 seconds, which is faster than the original GoogLeNet (283 minutes 50 seconds) and significantly faster than ResNet-50 (354 minutes 46 seconds).
While ResNet-50 achieved the highest Area Under Curve (AUC) score (0.7681) and Polygon Area Metric (PAM) score (0.7288), the proposed CNN achieved the best overall balance of accuracy and efficiency.

The study confirms that modifying GoogLeNet with a deconvolution layer can improve both diagnostic accuracy and computational efficiency for melanoma diagnosis compared to standard deep learning architectures.
important_remarks: The study is an extended version of a previous conference paper (Yilmaz & Trocan, 2020) and compares the proposed model against its own prior results rather than independent external benchmarks.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced with 19,373 benign cases versus 2,197 malignant cases (approx. 90% benign), which may bias the model towards the benign class.
The proposed model exhibits low specificity (0.5513), indicating a high false positive rate where benign lesions are frequently misclassified as malignant.
The study lacks external validation; all experiments were conducted on a single subset of the ISIC dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the marginal performance differences between the proposed model and baselines like ResNet-50.
The hardware used for training (NVIDIA GeForce GT 750M) is an older, consumer-grade GPU, which may not reflect performance on modern clinical infrastructure.
The paper does not report the specific data leakage prevention methods or cross-validation strategies beyond a single 80/20 train/validation split.
The authors acknowledge that the current model is a preliminary step towards an online teledermatology system, but no clinical deployment or user study is reported.
